lectureTitle: SAML (Security Assertion Markup Language) and Self-Sovereign Identities

examQuestions.promptVerbatim: Explain the concept and principles of Single Sign-On. What are the pros and cons?
Discuss the working principles of SAML (Security Assertion Markup Language), its architecture, and typical use cases.
Discuss different types of SAML (Security Assertion Markup Language) assertions and their content.

drills.question: A user must access dozens of cloud and internal apps daily, each with its own password. What is Single Sign-On and what core principle does it apply to fix this?
keyBeats: Log in once
Reuse authentication across services
Circle of trust between IdP (Identity Provider) and SPs (Service Providers)
No re-authentication for further services
drills.modelAnswer: Single Sign-On lets a user authenticate once with an identity provider and then access multiple services without logging in again. The user authenticates once to the IdP (Identity Provider), which issues a token carrying an assertion; the user can then access further services from other service providers in the same circle of trust without re-authenticating. User attributes may be shared with all members of the circle.

drills.question: What is the 'Circle of Trust' that SSO (Single Sign-On) depends on, and which two trust relationships make it work?
keyBeats: Business + operational agreements between IdP (Identity Provider) and SPs (Service Providers)
Identity info handled per agreed policies
IdP-SP trust agreement defines relationship
Separate user-IdP circle of trust
drills.modelAnswer: SSO (Single Sign-On) relies on a circle of trust between an identity provider and several service providers that have business relationships and operational agreements, within which identity information is handled confidentially per agreed policies. There must be an agreement between IdP (Identity Provider) and SP (Service Provider) defining their trust relationship. Separately, the user and IdP form another circle of trust: the user trusts the IdP to store her identities and use them properly.

drills.question: An IT (Information Technology) manager asks why SSO (Single Sign-On)/federation is worth deploying. What are the main pros for users, IT and the business?
keyBeats: Users: no battery of passwords, click-and-work
IT (Information Technology): better security + lower help-desk burden
Business: faster sharing, compliance, less risk
Passwords never cross the firewall
drills.modelAnswer: Users love it because internet SSO (Single Sign-On) frees them from remembering and resetting many passwords, giving click-and-work access. IT (Information Technology) loves it because it simultaneously enhances security and reduces the support/help-desk burden. Business leaders love it because it accelerates application and data sharing with partners while decreasing risk and increasing regulatory compliance. With SAML (Security Assertion Markup Language) the user authenticates at their own IdP (Identity Provider), so in this browser-redirect/IdP-initiated SSO model the user password never crosses the corporate firewall to the service provider, eliminating opportunities for identity theft and phishing.
drills.citation: saml-101-white-paper.txt PAGE 06 (Users/IT/Business pros); firewall/identity-theft/phishing point on IAM (Identity and Access Management) lecture slide PAGE 12 and saml-101-white-paper.txt PAGE 09

drills.question: What are the cons and limitations of SSO (Single Sign-On)/SAML (Security Assertion Markup Language), and how do newer protocols address them?
keyBeats: SAML (Security Assertion Markup Language) never optimised for native mobile apps
Weak for API (Application Programming Interface)-driven multi-source apps
Single point of compromise concern
OAuth 2.0 / OpenID Connect successors
drills.modelAnswer: The major limitation is that SAML (Security Assertion Markup Language) was never optimised to enable SSO (Single Sign-On) for native mobile applications or for apps consolidating data via API (Application Programming Interface) calls from many third-party sources. OAuth 2.0 and OpenID Connect (which builds on OAuth) emerged to meet these needs, becoming key standards for mobile and machine-to-machine interactions. Conceptually SSO also concentrates risk: if the single IdP (Identity Provider) login is compromised, access to many apps is exposed, which is why strong authentication at the IdP matters.

drills.question: Where does SSO (Single Sign-On) sit in the four-phase evolution of identity management, and what comes after it?
keyBeats: Phase 1 centralized
Phase 2 federated = SSO (Single Sign-On)
Phase 3 user-centric detour
Phase 4 self-sovereign / decentralized
drills.modelAnswer: Christopher Allen's model has four phases: centralized identity, federated identity, user-centric identity, and self-sovereign identity. SSO (Single Sign-On) corresponds to the federated phase, where administrative control is held by multiple federated authorities and users can use the same identity across multiple sites. Federated IDMS (Identity Management System) (SAML (Security Assertion Markup Language), OpenID Connect) is still dominant, but decentralized/self-sovereign identity is the next step and is rapidly gaining momentum.

drills.question: A colleague asks 'what exactly is SAML (Security Assertion Markup Language)?' How would you define it, including who standardised it and which version dominates?
keyBeats: XML (Extensible Markup Language)-based framework for security/identity info
OASIS (Organization for the Advancement of Structured Information Standards) standard, roots in Liberty Alliance
SAML (Security Assertion Markup Language) 2.0 approved March 2005
Exchanges auth + authz between security domains
drills.modelAnswer: SAML (Security Assertion Markup Language) is an open, XML (Extensible Markup Language)-based framework for exchanging security and identity management information, developed by OASIS (Organization for the Advancement of Structured Information Standards) (with roots in the Liberty Alliance / Shibboleth / ID-FF convergence). Version 2.0 was approved in March 2005 and is the dominant version. It enables the secure exchange of authentication and authorization information between security domains such as separate organisations or company divisions, and notably never requires a third party to access the user's credentials.

drills.question: What are the two main entity roles in SAML (Security Assertion Markup Language) architecture, and what does each do?
keyBeats: IdP (Identity Provider) = SAML (Security Assertion Markup Language) authority / asserting party
IdP authenticates user, issues assertions
SP (Service Provider) = relying party that consumes assertions
SP uses assertions for access control
drills.modelAnswer: SAML (Security Assertion Markup Language) has two roles. The SAML authority / asserting party is the identity provider (IdP), responsible for maintaining and authenticating the user's identity (usually via an IdM (Identity Management) system) and producing assertions. The relying party / service provider (SP) is the system entity that uses received assertions; typically several SPs make use of assertions about a subject in order to control access and provide a customised service.

drills.question: Walk through the steps of an SP (Service Provider)-initiated SAML (Security Assertion Markup Language) SSO (Single Sign-On) flow from the lecture.
keyBeats: 1. User authenticates once with IdP (Identity Provider)
2. User requests RP (Relying Party) resource
3. RP asks for assertion
4. IdP composes + signs assertion, sends to RP
5. RP verifies DSIG (Digital Signature), makes access decision
drills.modelAnswer: In the SP (Service Provider)-initiated flow the lecture lists ordered steps: (1) the user authenticates once with the IdP (Identity Provider) (Single Sign-On); (2) the user requests access to the relying party's resources; (3) the RP (Relying Party) asks for an assertion to be provided; (4) the IdP composes the assertion, signs it with its digital signature and sends it to the RP; (5) the RP inspects the digital signature (DSIG) to verify the SAML (Security Assertion Markup Language) token's integrity and authenticity, then analyses its contents and makes access-control decisions. The SP-side endpoint receiving the assertion is the Assertion Consumer Service.

drills.question: Give the typical enterprise use cases for SAML (Security Assertion Markup Language) federation.
keyBeats: Outbound internet SSO (Single Sign-On) to cloud/partner/vendor apps
Inbound internet SSO for customers/partners to internal apps
Internal web SSO across subsidiaries/acquisitions
Bridges separate security domains / IdM (Identity Management) systems
drills.modelAnswer: Typical enterprise use cases are: outbound internet SSO (Single Sign-On) to web-based cloud, business-process-outsourcing, partner, vendor or supplier applications; inbound internet SSO to give customers, business partners and affiliates/subsidiaries secure access to internal web applications; and internal web SSO providing centralized access for the organisation and any subsidiaries, acquisitions or joint ventures where separate security domains, multiple federation standards or separate IdM (Identity Management) systems exist.

drills.question: How does SAML (Security Assertion Markup Language) protect assertions and messages cryptographically, and what guarantees does this provide?
keyBeats: Uses W3C (World Wide Web Consortium) XML (Extensible Markup Language) Signature (enveloped/enveloping/detached)
Optional XML Encryption for confidentiality
Signed assertion: integrity, origin auth, non-repudiation
Can be packaged in SOAP (Simple Object Access Protocol) tokens over untrusted networks
drills.modelAnswer: SAML (Security Assertion Markup Language) uses the W3C (World Wide Web Consortium) XML (Extensible Markup Language) Signature standard (which supports three node types: enveloped, enveloping, detached) and may also use XML Encryption for confidentiality. A SAML assertion signed by the asserting party supports integrity of the assertion, authentication of the asserting party to a relying party, and non-repudiation of origin if based on the authority's key pair; a signed protocol message gives equivalent guarantees for the message. Assertions can be packaged into encrypted security tokens, e.g. SOAP (Simple Object Access Protocol) messages, to be sent over an unprotected network.

drills.question: Why did SAML (Security Assertion Markup Language) become dominant over WS-Federation for B2B (Business-to-Business) federation?
keyBeats: OASIS (Organization for the Advancement of Structured Information Standards) standard since 2002, field-proven
Interoperability certification (Liberty/Kantara)
Thousands of production deployments
Even Microsoft added SAML (Security Assertion Markup Language) 2.0 to ADFS (Active Directory Federation Services)
drills.modelAnswer: SAML's (Security Assertion Markup Language's) major advantage is its widespread and growing adoption. As an OASIS (Organization for the Advancement of Structured Information Standards) standard since 2002 it enjoys field-proven security, scalability and dependability in thousands of production deployments, plus rigorous interoperability certification (Liberty Alliance / Kantara). WS-Federation, part of the Microsoft/IBM WS-* suite, stays popular only in Microsoft-centric ADFS (Active Directory Federation Services) environments. Significantly, Microsoft added SAML 2.0 support to ADFS, acknowledging SAML's dominance in B2B (Business-to-Business) identity federation.

drills.question: How many types of SAML (Security Assertion Markup Language) assertion statements are specified, and what does each one tell the Service Provider?
keyBeats: Exactly 3 types
Authentication <AuthnStatement>: how/when authenticated
Attribute <AttributeStatement>: identifying attributes
Authorization Decision <AuthzDecisionStatement>: what resource allowed
drills.modelAnswer: SAML (Security Assertion Markup Language) specifies three (and only three) assertion statement types. Authentication (<AuthnStatement>) tells the SP (Service Provider) that the subject was authenticated with the IdP (Identity Provider) using a particular method at a specific time, and may include authentication context. Attribute (<AttributeStatement>) informs the SP that the user has specific identifying attributes. Authorization Decision (<AuthzDecisionStatement>) tells the SP what resource a user is entitled to access based on certain evidence.

drills.question: Beyond the statement, what common information does every SAML assertion contain?
keyBeats: Issuer of the assertion
Issue time (IssueInstant)
Subject (<Subject> element)
Statement(s) + validity conditions
drills.modelAnswer: A SAML assertion is a package of information made by a SAML authority, usually about a subject represented by the <Subject> element. It contains the issuer of the assertion, the time it was issued, the subject, one or more statements about the subject, and the conditions under which the assertion is valid. These statements are what service providers use for access-control decisions.

drills.question: In the lecture's AuthnStatement XML example, what does the assertion actually state about the subject?
keyBeats: Subject NameID jygH5F90I
Authenticated at AuthnInstant time
AuthnContextClassRef = PasswordProtectedTransport
Issuer + signature wrap the assertion
drills.modelAnswer: The example asserts that the subject (NameID 'jygH5F90I') was authenticated at the given AuthnInstant using the Authentication Context Class named 'Password Protected Transport' (urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:2.0:ac:classes:PasswordProtectedTransport). The assertion is wrapped with version, ID, IssueInstant, the <saml:Issuer>, and a <ds:Signature>, with the claim carried inside the <saml:AuthnStatement> / <saml:AuthnContext> elements.

drills.question: In the SOAP/attribute example, how is an attribute like eduPersonAffiliation represented, and what limits the assertion's validity?
keyBeats: AttributeStatement holds Attribute Name + values
eduPersonAffiliation = member, staff
Conditions: NotBefore / NotOnOrAfter window
AudienceRestriction limits intended SP
drills.modelAnswer: Inside the <saml:AttributeStatement>, an <saml:Attribute> (e.g. FriendlyName 'eduPersonAffiliation', a urn:oid name) carries one or more <saml:AttributeValue> elements such as 'member' and 'staff'. Validity is bounded by the <saml:Conditions> element with NotBefore / NotOnOrAfter timestamps, and an <saml:AudienceRestriction>/<saml:Audience> limiting which SP may consume it. SubjectConfirmation (e.g. method bearer) ties the assertion to the subject and recipient.

drills.question: How do SAML assertions relate to the broader claims/assertions/credentials concepts from earlier lectures (Q2c)?
keyBeats: RP requests a claim from the user
User asks IdP to make an assertion to the claim
Assertion = signed statement backing a claim
Claim = (package of) attributes about a subject
drills.modelAnswer: An assertion is the IdP's signed statement backing a claim. In the SSO model the RP requests a claim from the user, the user asks the IdP to make an assertion to that claim, and the user presents the asserted claim (claim + assertion) to the RP. A claim is a (package of) attributes describing a subject; the SAML attribute assertion is precisely the mechanism that lets a trusted authority vouch for those attribute claims so the SP can make access decisions.

drills.question: What is a Verifiable Credential (VC), and what makes it trustworthy in the SSI (Self-Sovereign Identity) model?
keyBeats: Set of claims issued by an Issuer to a Holder
Tamper-evident with cryptographic authorship
Includes metadata: issuer, expiry, key, revocation
Proof = Issuer's digital signature
drills.modelAnswer: A Verifiable Credential (VC) is a set of one or more claims made by an Issuer about a subject (Holder); it is a tamper-evident credential whose authorship can be cryptographically verified. It contains the claims plus metadata (issuer, expiry date/time, a public key for verification, the revocation mechanism) and a proof supplied by the Issuer such as a digital signature. This lets a Verifier confirm who issued it and that it was not tampered with, without contacting the Issuer.

drills.question: Describe the three key SSI (Self-Sovereign Identity) roles (Issuer, Holder, Verifier) and how a Verifiable Presentation flows between them.
keyBeats: Issuer issues VCs (Verifiable Credentials) to Holder
Holder stores VCs in wallet, composes VPs (Verifiable Presentations)
Verifier checks VP (Verifiable Presentation) without asking Issuer
Verifier resolves Issuer's DID (Decentralized Identifier) Document
drills.modelAnswer: The Issuer issues Verifiable Credentials (VCs) on demand to a subject (Holder). The Holder keeps a collection of VCs in a Digital Identity Wallet and can compose a Verifiable Presentation (VP) from one or more VCs to show a Verifier on demand. The Verifier (typically a service provider asking for user info) can verify the statements in the VP and which party made them without asking the Issuer; instead it verifies the Issuer's signature by checking the Issuer's DID (Decentralized Identifier) Document. This puts Holders back in control of their data.

drills.question: What is a Decentralized Identifier (DID), and what are its four core properties?
keyBeats: Globally unique identifier, text string in 3 parts
Scheme : DID (Decentralized Identifier) method : method-specific id
Persistent, resolvable, cryptographically-verifiable, decentralized
Private key proves ownership; no central authority
drills.modelAnswer: A DID (Decentralized Identifier) is a new type of globally unique identifier (a URI (Uniform Resource Identifier)) that associates a DID subject with a DID document. It is a text string of three parts: the DID URI scheme identifier, the DID method, and the method-specific identifier (e.g. did:example:123456789abcdefghi). Its four core properties are: permanent/persistent, resolvable (look it up for metadata), cryptographically-verifiable (prove control with cryptography), and decentralized (no central registration authority). The private key proves ownership.

drills.question: How does the Verifiable Data Registry act as the trust mechanism in SSI (Self-Sovereign Identity), and how does selective/minimum disclosure protect privacy?
keyBeats: Registry = distributed ledger, immutable once recorded
Issuer writes DID (Decentralized Identifier) + public key; Verifier resolves it
Holder selects only requested info into a VP (Verifiable Presentation)
Minimum disclosure / SD-JWTs (Selective Disclosure JSON Web Tokens), ZKP (Zero-Knowledge Proof) techniques
drills.modelAnswer: The Verifiable Data Registry is typically a distributed ledger (Sovrin, IOTA, Ethereum, Hyperledger Besu); once a DID (Decentralized Identifier) and DID document are registered they become immutable, and a Verifier can resolve the DID document to get the subject's public key and the Issuer's DID. For privacy, the Holder selects only the requested information from one or more VCs (Verifiable Credentials), makes transformations, and composes a Verifiable Presentation (VP) as a minimum-disclosure claim. Selective Disclosure JWTs (JSON Web Tokens) (SD-JWTs (Selective Disclosure JSON Web Tokens)) and zero-knowledge techniques realise this.

drills.question: Contrast the SSO (Single Sign-On)/federated trust model with the SSI (Self-Sovereign Identity) trust model. What is the fundamental shift?
keyBeats: Federated: IdP (Identity Provider) is intermediary in every transaction
SSI: trust triangle Issuer-Holder-Verifier
Verifier need not contact Issuer (uses ledger)
User/Holder controls and stores own data
drills.modelAnswer: In the federated/SSO (Single Sign-On) model an identity provider sits between user and service provider and makes assertions in each transaction. SSI (Self-Sovereign Identity) uses two different trust models: a trust triangle where an Issuer issues credentials to a Holder, the Holder sends presentations to a Verifier, and all register/verify identifiers via a verifiable data registry. The fundamental shift is that the Verifier can verify cryptographically against the ledger without contacting the Issuer, and the Holder (user) controls and stores their own data rather than depending on an IdP (Identity Provider).

exercises.prompt: Discuss the information flow in Single Sign-On systems and SAML (Security Assertion Markup Language) in particular!
Discuss the detailed content of SAML (Security Assertion Markup Language) assertions! Which technologies are used for composing assertions?
Discuss the pros and cons of Single Sign-On systems!
Explain the basic idea of Self-Sovereign Identities! What are some of the barriers for introducing SSI (Self-Sovereign Identity)?
What is the difference between SSO (Single Sign-On) and SSI (Self-Sovereign Identity) systems? What do we want to achieve?
What are DIDs (Decentralized Identifiers) and DID documents? How are they used?
What are Verifiable Credentials and Verifiable Presentations? How are they used?
